 INTELLIGENCE BRIEFING — As of 2025-04-08
 Subject: Donald J. Trump
 Knowledge Window: 2025-01-01 → 2025-04-08
 Events in window: 358 | Showing top 8
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

[2025-01-31] President Trump Issues Executive Order 'Unleashing Prosperity Through Deregulation' to Reduce Federal Regulation
President Donald J. Trump issued an executive order titled "Unleashing Prosperity Through Deregulation" on January 31, 2025. The order mandates that federal agencies identify and repeal at least 10 existing rules, regulations, or guidance documents for every new one they issue. For fiscal year 2025, the order requires that the total incremental cost of all new regulations, including repealed ones, must be less than zero. The Director of the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) is tasked with standardizing the measurement and estimation of regulatory costs and implementing the order. Regulations concerning the military, national security, homeland security, foreign affairs, immigration, and agency organization/management/personnel are exempt. The order also revokes the 2023 update to OMB Circular A-4, reinstating the 2003 version, which changes discount rates to 3% and 7% and removes directives to consider effects outside the U.S. and emphasize distributional effects. Starting FY 2026, agencies must report regulatory costs/savings, and the OMB Director will set an incremental cost allowance for each agency.
Key facts:
  • 10-to-1 deregulation initiative launched
  • Administrative Procedure Act (APA) mentioned as relevant law
  • Agencies must identify 10 existing rules for repeal for each new rule
  • Claimed $1.7 trillion in costs imposed by Biden Administration
  • Director of Office of Management and Budget to standardize regulatory cost measurement
  • Director of the Office of Management and Budget responsible for implementation
  • Directs OMB to reinstate the 2003 version of Circular A-4
  • Executive Order signed by President Donald J. Trump
  • Executive Order titled "Unleashing Prosperity Through Deregulation"
  • Exemptions include military, national security, homeland security, foreign affairs, immigration, and agency internal regulations
  • Exempts regulations related to military, national security, homeland security, foreign affairs, immigration, and agency organization or personnel
  • First Trump Administration eliminated 5.5 regulations for every new one
  • First Trump Administration's 2-for-1 regulatory reduction goal
  • Fiscal year 2025 cost target for new regulations: "significantly less than zero"
  • Fiscal year 2025 total incremental cost of new regulations must be significantly less than zero
  • Issued January 31, 2025
  • Issued on January 31, 2025
  • More ambitious than the "2 for 1" policy of EO 13771
  • Previous challenges to the "2 for 1" order (Public Citizen v. Trump, California v. Trump) were unsuccessful
  • Reinstates prior discount rates of 3% and 7% (from 2%)
  • Removes directive for agencies to consider effects outside of the United States
  • Removes emphasis on distributional effects of regulations
  • Requires agencies to identify at least 10 existing regulations for repeal when proposing a new one ("10 for 1" tradeoff)
  • Requires repeal of at least 10 existing regulations for each new one
  • Revokes the 2023 update to OMB Circular A-4
  • Supreme Court’s decision in Loper Bright mentioned as relevant legal context
  • Total incremental cost of all new regulations for FY 2025 must be "significantly less than zero"
  • White House Office of Management and Budget (OMB) Director to provide guidance and set annual regulatory cost allowances for agencies
Sources: www.whitehouse.gov, www.lathamreg.com, www.sidley.com (3 sources, verified date)

[2025-03-19] Trump Urges Federal Reserve to Cut Interest Rates to Support Tariff Plans
The Federal Reserve announced Wednesday it would maintain its key federal funds rate at 4.5%. The central bank cited rising economic uncertainty, attributing it partly to President Donald Trump's tariff agenda. The Fed lowered its forecast for gross domestic product (GDP) for the year to 1.7% from 2.1% and projected a key inflation measure at 3% from 2%. Eighteen of 19 policymakers saw an increased risk of GDP decline. The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) maintained rates but indicated two rate reductions are likely by year-end.

Following the announcement, former President Donald Trump posted on Truth Social, urging Chair Jerome Powell and the FOMC to cut interest rates to ease the economy's transition to U.S. tariffs. Trump's post also referenced April 2 as a date for revealing global trade study results, which could lead to further tariffs. Powell acknowledged tariffs as a factor in accelerating price increases, stating the impact would likely be "transitory."
Key facts:
  • 11 policymakers anticipate unemployment rate could climb to 4.5% this year
  • 18 of 19 policymakers see increased risk of GDP falling
  • April 2nd referenced for global trade study results and potential new tariffs
  • Donald Trump
  • FOMC indicated two rate reductions likely by year-end
  • Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC)
  • Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell
  • Federal funds rate maintained at 4.5%
  • GDP forecast lowered from 2.1% to 1.7% for the rest of the year
  • Jerome Powell
  • Key inflation measure forecast closer to 3% (previously 2%)
  • Key interest rate kept steady (4.25%-4.5%)
  • Omair Sharif, managing director of Inflation Insights consultancy
  • President Donald Trump's tariff agenda
  • S&P 500 slipped into correction territory (10% drop) last week
  • Seema Shah, chief global strategist at Principal Asset Management
  • Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent
  • Truth Social post
  • Whitney Watson, managing director at Goldman Sachs
"The Fed would be MUCH better off CUTTING RATES as U.S. Tariffs start to transition (ease!) their way into the economy. Do the right thing. April 2nd is Liberation Day in America!!!" — Donald Trump (Truth Social post)
"I think that's kind of the base case. But as I said, we really can't know that. We're going to have to see how things actually work out" — Jerome Powell (Post-FOMC meeting news conference, regarding the impact of tariffs)
"Uncertainty around the economic outlook has increased" — Federal Reserve statement (Official statement released by the Fed)
"Inflation has started to move up now, we think, partly in response to tariffs, and there may be a delay in further progress in the course of this year" — Jerome Powell (News conference after the Fed statement was released)
"The Fed is as lost in the wilderness as the rest of us trying to decipher the continual shifts in economic policy from 1600 Pennsylvania Avenue" — Omair Sharif (Note to clients following Wednesday’s rate decision)
"Revisions to [policymakers'] projections had a somewhat 'stagflationary' feel with forecasts for growth and inflation moving in opposite directions" — Whitney Watson (Note to clients on Wednesday afternoon)
"For the time being the Fed is in wait and see mode, as it monitors whether the recent growth slowdown develops into something more serious" — Whitney Watson (Note to clients on Wednesday afternoon)
"rate cuts will be necessary in the near future — they just need a full set of information to give them the green light" — Seema Shah (Commentary on the Fed's revised projections)
"The Fed saw downside risks to growth and upside risks to unemployment, but they continued to power ahead with rate hikes" — Omair Sharif (Referring to policymakers' approach in 2022)
"They’re saying now that they will err on the side of making sure inflation does not get out of hand again" — Omair Sharif (Commentary on the Fed's current stance)
Sources: www.cnbc.com, www.nbcnews.com (2 sources)

[2025-03-04] President Trump Delivers 2025 State of the Union Address to Joint Session of Congress
President Donald Trump delivered his 2025 State of the Union address to a joint session of the 119th United States Congress on March 4, 2025. The address, his first of a second non-consecutive term, began at 9:19 p.m. EST in the House chamber of the U.S. Capitol and lasted 1 hour and 39 minutes. Vice President J.D. Vance and Speaker of the House Mike Johnson presided. Trump outlined proposals including border security measures, economic plans such as extending the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act and eliminating taxes on tips, and enhanced domestic energy production. He also announced plans to withdraw from international agreements including the Paris Climate Accord and the World Health Organization, and orders making English the official U.S. language and banning men from women's sports. The speech drew an estimated 36.6 million viewers. Secretary of Veterans Affairs Doug Collins was the designated survivor. Democratic Representative Al Green was removed and later censured for disrupting the speech.
Key facts:
  • 119th United States Congress
  • 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act
  • 25% tariffs on imported aluminum, copper, lumber, and steel
  • Addressed a joint session of the 119th United States Congress
  • Authorized expansion of oil and gas production ('drill, baby, drill')
  • CHIPS and Science Act (mentioned for repeal)
  • Claimed DOGE identified $1.9 billion waste for 'decarbonization of homes committee' linked to Stacey Abrams
  • Claimed DOGE identified $22 billion waste from HHS for housing/cars for 'illegal aliens'
  • Claimed DOGE identified $45 million waste for DEI scholarships in Burma
  • Claimed DOGE identified $8 million waste for 'making mice transgender'
  • Claimed actions in 43 days: Nearly 100 Executive Orders, over 400 Executive Actions
  • Congressman Sylvester Turner made his last public appearance.
  • Created Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE)
  • Date: March 4, 2025
  • Declared national emergency on southern border
  • Declared national energy emergency
  • Democratic Representative Al Green removed and censured for disruption
  • Deployed U.S. military and Border Patrol to southern border
  • Designated survivor: Secretary of Veterans Affairs Doug Collins
  • Donald Trump, 47th President of the United States
  • Duration: 1 hour, 39 minutes (longest address to a joint session in at least 61 years)
  • Elon Musk appointed head of DOGE
  • Estimated $500 billion in annual waste, fraud, and abuse in government
  • Estimated 36.6 million television viewers in the U.S.
  • Estimated 36.6 million viewers
  • Event: 2025 Joint Address to Congress
  • Executive order announced to rename Anahuac National Wildlife Refuge to Jocelyn Nungaray National Wildlife Refuge

  • Four Supreme Court justices attended: Chief Justice Roberts, Associate Justices Elena Kagan, Brett Kavanaugh, and Amy Coney Barrett.
  • Gold Card program for foreign investors contributing $5 million

  • Representative Tim Walberg (R-MI)

  • Signed executive order to ban men from playing in women's sports

  • Speaker of the House Mike Johnson
  • Speaker: Donald Trump
  • Speech duration: Over 1 hour and 40 minutes
  • Time: 9:19 p.m. EST to 10:59 p.m. EST
  • Trump's claim on immigration: "21 million people poured into the United States" during Biden administration. Fact-checkers reported 10.4, 10.8, or 14 million apprehended. Congressional Republicans estimated at least 2 million unapprehended.
  • US aid to Ukraine: Congressional records show ~$175 billion; Kiel Institute figures: US allocated $121 billion + $5 billion committed; European countries contributed ~$140 billion + $122 billion committed.
  • United Nations Human Rights Council (mentioned for withdrawal)
  • United States Capitol
  • Venue: House Chamber, U.S. Capitol, Washington, D.C.

"America is back." — Donald Trump (Opening of the 2025 State of the Union address)
"President Trump's message of making America secure and competitive again are welcome words for the people of Michigan." — Representative Tim Walberg (R-MI) (Post-speech reaction to the 2025 State of the Union address)
"We have accomplished more in 43 days than most administrations accomplished in 4 years, 8 years — and we are just getting started." — Donald Trump (2025 Joint Address to Congress)
"Within hours of taking the oath of office, I declared a national emergency on our southern border, and I deployed the U.S. military and Border Patrol to repel the invasion of our country, and what a job they've done." — Donald Trump (2025 Joint Address to Congress)
"And two days ago I signed an order making English the official language of the United States of America." — Donald Trump (2025 Joint Address to Congress)

"Do you want to keep it going for another five years?" — Donald Trump (Questioning continued U.S. funding for Ukraine during his speech)
"Yeah, yeah, you would say, 'Pocahontas says yes.'" — Donald Trump (Referring to Senator Elizabeth Warren during discussion of Ukraine funding)
"I don't know why we can't fully celebrate [...] I think that's something we could all celebrate." — John Fetterman (Fox News interview on March 6, regarding the DJ Daniel incident during the speech)
"quite insulting" — Lejone Mpotjoane (Criticizing Trump's remark referring to Lesotho as a nation 'which nobody has ever heard of')

[2025-01-20] Trump Issues Executive Orders for Federal Employee Hiring Freeze and Return to In-Person Work
President Trump, following his inauguration on January 20, 2025, issued a series of executive actions. He directed the Secretary of State to align U.S. foreign policy with an "America First" directive, prioritizing American interests and citizens. On immigration, actions included "realigning" the Refugee Admission Program, designating cartels as foreign terrorist organizations, declaring a national emergency at the southern border, and directing construction of a border wall. The administration also moved to end "catch and release," reinstate "Remain in Mexico," and suspend refugee resettlement from January 27.

Regarding federal employees, Trump mandated all executive branch personnel return to in-person, full-time work, ending remote arrangements. He also imposed a 90-day hiring freeze on federal civilian employees, effective January 20, 2025, for unfilled positions, with exemptions for national security and immigration enforcement. The freeze excludes military personnel and prohibits using third-party contractors to bypass it. Additionally, Trump initiated actions to withdraw the U.S. from the Paris Agreement, declared a national energy emergency, and temporarily halted new wind energy projects on federal lands and waters. These orders also paused funding from the Inflation Reduction Act and Bipartisan Infrastructure Law.
Key facts:
  • "Realigning" the Refugee Admission Program
  • Agencies involved in workforce reduction plan: Office of Management and Budget (OMB), Office of Personnel Management (OPM), United States DOGE Service (USDS)
  • America First Policy Directive

"Our southern border is overrun by cartels, criminal gangs, known terrorists, human traffickers, smugglers, unvetted military-age males from foreign adversaries, and illicit narcotics that harm Americans" — Donald Trump (via signed order) (Order declaring a national emergency at the southern border)
"it is the policy of the United States to ensure that the Armed Forces of the United States prioritize the protection of the sovereignty and territorial integrity of the United States along our national borders." — Donald Trump (via signed order) (Order clarifying the military's role in border security)
"suspending the physical entry of aliens involved in an invasion into the United States across the southern border until I determine that the invasion has concluded" — Donald Trump (via proclamation) (Proclamation guaranteeing states protection against invasion)
"What this particular action does is it rescinds the open borders policies of the Biden administration and equips agents and officers of ICE and CBP with the authorities they need to deport people from the U.S. It also reaffirms the faithful execution of the immigration laws, prioritizes enforcement actions against criminal illegal aliens and establishes federal Homeland Security Task Forces to cooperate with state and local law enforcement in the removal of gangs, criminals and illegal aliens from the United States." — White House officials (Call with reporters previewing actions)
"So as you know, this is about national security, this is about public safety, and this is about the victims of some of the most violent, abusive criminals we've seen enter our country in our lifetime. And it ends today." — White House officials (Call with reporters previewing actions)
"We will drill, baby, drill" — Donald Trump (Inauguration speech on Jan. 20)
Sources: www.whitehouse.gov, www.npr.org, www.npr.org, www.foley.com (4 sources, verified date)

[2025-01-20] President Issues Memorandum Ordering Regulatory Freeze Pending Review for Executive Departments and Agencies
On January 20, 2025, President Trump issued a memorandum titled "Regulatory Freeze Pending Review" to the heads of executive departments and agencies. The directive ordered an immediate halt to proposing or issuing new rules without approval from department or agency heads appointed after January 20, 2025. Agencies were instructed to withdraw any rules sent to the Office of the Federal Register (OFR) but not yet published. The memorandum also directed agencies to consider postponing for 60 days the effective date of rules already published or issued but not yet in effect, to allow for review of factual, legal, and policy questions. The freeze applies to 'rules' as defined by the Administrative Procedure Act and Executive Order 12866. Exceptions are granted for emergency situations or rules subject to statutory or judicial deadlines, with the Director or Acting Director of the Office of Management and Budget authorized to grant such exemptions and oversee implementation. Similar regulatory freezes have been issued by previous administrations.
Key facts:
  • "Rule" definition includes regulatory actions (Executive Order 12866) and guidance documents (Executive Order 13891)
  • "Rules" defined under Administrative Procedure Act (5 U.S.C. § 551(4)) and Executive Order 12866
  • 60-day postponement consideration for effective dates of rules
  • Addressed to: Heads of Executive Departments and Agencies
  • Directed all federal agencies to place a freeze on new or pending federal rules
  • Directive issued by the White House
  • Directs consideration of 60-day postponement for effective dates of published/issued but not yet effective rules
  • Document Number: 2025-01906
  • Does not impact regulations already in effect or stop work on new regulations
  • Exceptions for emergency situations or rules subject to statutory or judicial deadlines
  • Issued to all executive departments and agencies
  • Mandates withdrawal of unpublished rules from the Office of the Federal Register (OFR)

  • Memorandum issued on January 20, 2025

  • Requires review and approval of new rules by department/agency heads appointed after noon on January 20, 2025
  • Section 14(c) of the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA)
  • Similar freezes issued by President Biden (2021), President Trump (2017), and President Obama (2009)

"for the purpose of reviewing any questions of fact, law, and policy the rules may raise" — White House directive (Regarding postponing the effective date of rules)

Sources: www.cov.com, www.federalregister.gov, www.lawandtheworkplace.com (3 sources, verified date)

[2025-01-20] President Issues Memorandum Declaring OECD Global Tax Deal Has No Force or Effect in US
On January 20, 2025, President Donald Trump delivered his second inaugural address, outlining plans for executive orders related to deportations, energy, and tariffs.

On the same day, the President issued a memorandum stating that prior administration commitments regarding the Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) Global Tax Deal have no force or effect in the United States unless adopted by Congress. The memorandum directed the Treasury Secretary and the U.S. Permanent Representative to the OECD to formally notify the organization of this position. It also instructed the Treasury Secretary, in consultation with the United States Trade Representative, to investigate foreign countries for non-compliance with tax treaties or for implementing extraterritorial tax rules on American companies, requiring a list of responsive actions within 60 days.

Additionally, the administration issued Executive Order 14154, “Unleashing American Energy,” directing federal agencies to immediately pause the disbursement of funds appropriated through the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 and the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act. This has resulted in slowed funding for various programs and subsequent litigation.
Key facts:
  • 11% of Black voters for Trump (exit polls)
  • 46% of Latino voters for Trump (exit polls)
  • 60-day deadline for recommendations to the President
  • 62% disapproved of Trump pardoning Jan. 6 defendants (NPR poll)

Sources: www.npr.org, www.whitehouse.gov, www.arnoldporter.com (3 sources, verified date)

[2025-01-20] President Trump Issues Executive Orders Reshaping Federal Environmental and Energy Policy on First Day of Second Term
On January 20, 2025, President Donald Trump issued executive orders, memoranda, and proclamations. Actions included a presidential proclamation declaring an "invasion" at the southern border, suspending migrant entry and asylum requests. Executive Order 14165 "Securing Our Borders" reinstated the "Remain in Mexico" program, restarted border wall construction, and limited humanitarian parole. A national emergency was declared at the border, directing military deployment. An executive order also sought to reinterpret the 14th Amendment regarding birthright citizenship, facing immediate legal challenges.

In energy and environmental policy, Executive Order 14154 "Unleashing American Energy" promoted fossil fuel production and critical mineral development, and ended federal support for electric vehicles. The U.S. withdrew from the Paris Agreement and the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change. A national energy emergency was declared to expedite fossil fuel projects.

Regarding federal operations, a hiring freeze was implemented across most executive branch agencies. A "Regulatory Freeze Pending Review" was enacted for new rules. Federal employees were directed to return to in-person work. An executive order defined sex as an immutable biological classification, directing agencies to remove materials promoting "gender ideology." The administration also established an "America First Trade Policy" and revoked 58 executive orders and 10 presidential memoranda from the previous administration, targeting diversity, equity, and inclusion policies.
Key facts:
  • 1,500 active-duty troops (1,000 U.S. Army, 500 Marines) preparing for deployment as of January 22
  • 10 immigration-related executive orders signed
  • 12-month timeline for US exit from WHO

"It is the policy of the United States to take all appropriate action to secure the borders of our Nation through the following means: (a) Establishing a physical wall and other barriers monitored and supported by adequate personnel and technology; (b) Deterring and preventing the entry of illegal aliens into the United States; (c) Detaining, to the maximum extent authorized by law, aliens apprehended on suspicion of violating Federal or State law, until such time as they are removed from the United States; (d) Removing promptly all aliens who enter or remain in violation of Federal law; (e) Pursuing criminal charges against illegal aliens who violate the immigration laws, and against those who facilitate their unlawful presence in the United States; (f) Cooperating fully with State and local law enforcement officials in enacting Federal-State partnerships to enforce Federal immigration priorities; and (g) Obtaining complete operational control of the borders of the United States." — Executive Order 14165 (Section 2 (Policy) of Executive Order 14165)

[2025-04-08] President Trump Signs Executive Orders on Electric Grid Reliability, State Energy Policies, and Coal Industry
President Donald J. Trump signed three executive orders on April 8, 2025. One order, titled "Protecting American Energy from State Overreach," directs the U.S. Attorney General to identify state and local laws, regulations, and policies that burden domestic energy resources and are deemed unconstitutional, preempted by federal law, or otherwise unenforceable. The order targets laws related to climate change, environmental, social, and governance (ESG) initiatives, environmental justice, carbon emissions, and carbon penalties or taxes. The Attorney General is mandated to take action to stop the enforcement of such laws and submit a report to the President by June 7, 2025. California Governor Gavin Newsom opposed the order. The California legislature allocated $25 million to counter federal environmental efforts. Another order, "Strengthening the Reliability and Security of the United States Electric Grid," directs the Secretary of Energy to streamline procedures for assessing reserve margins and ensuring critical generation interconnection. A third order, "Reinvigorating America’s Beautiful Clean Coal Industry," prioritizes coal redevelopment, including for AI data centers.
Key facts:
  • 30-day deadline for DOE to develop reserve margin methodology
  • 50 MW nameplate capacity threshold for generation resources
  • 60-day deadline for Attorney General's report (June 7, 2025)
  • 60-day deadline for U.S. Attorney General's status report
  • 90-day deadline for DOE to publish reserve margin methodology

  • Attorney General to take action to stop enforcement of identified illegal state laws
  • California Governor: Gavin Newsom
  • California laws mentioned: SB-253, SB-261
  • California legislature allocated $25 million to fight federal efforts
